nudging.definition: Gently pushing or encouraging someone toward something.
nudging.example: Sleep nudging helps babies learn to sleep without force.

tolerance.definition: The ability to accept or deal with something difficult.
tolerance.example: Each child has a different tolerance for change.

secure.example: Make sure your child feels secure throughout the process.

attempt.example: Do not attempt sleep nudging before six months.

emotional.example: Emotional development is very important in the first year.

processing.definition: Working through or dealing with information or feelings.
processing.example: The brain is processing new experiences all the time.

foundation.definition: The base or starting point that everything else is built on.
foundation.example: Love and care are the foundation of good mental health.

wiring.definition: Forming connections, especially in the brain.
wiring.example: A baby's brain is wiring up through daily interactions.

interaction.definition: The way people act and communicate with each other.
interaction.example: Loving interaction helps a baby's brain grow.

permanence.definition: The state of lasting forever or for a very long time.
permanence.example: Object permanence means knowing something exists even when hidden.

psychotherapists.definition: Professionals who help people with mental and emotional problems through talking.

ingrained.definition: So deeply part of you that it is hard to change.
ingrained.example: Our habits become ingrained over many years.

overlook.definition: To not notice something or to forget about it.
overlook.example: It's easy to overlook how much babies need to learn.

cognitively.definition: Related to thinking and understanding with the mind.
cognitively.example: A child may understand something cognitively but not feel it.

bodily.example: Some feelings are experienced on a bodily level.

separation.example: Gradual separation is better than sudden change.

breastfeeding.definition: Feeding a baby milk from the mother's breast.
breastfeeding.example: Breastfeeding can help a baby feel safe and calm.

nursed.definition: Fed a baby with breast milk; also, cared for gently.
nursed.example: The baby was nursed back to sleep each time she woke.

baseline.definition: The starting point or lowest level that things are compared to.
baseline.example: Find your baby's comfort baseline before making changes.

drowsy.definition: Feeling sleepy and almost falling asleep.
drowsy.example: Stop nursing when the baby is drowsy but not yet asleep.

cuddle.definition: To hold someone close in your arms to show love.
cuddle.example: A warm cuddle can help a baby feel safe.

heartbeat.definition: The regular sound or movement of the heart pumping blood.
heartbeat.example: Babies are calmed by the sound of a heartbeat.

forehead.definition: The flat part of your face above your eyes.
forehead.example: She gently stroked the baby's forehead.

cot.definition: A small bed with high sides for a baby.
cot.example: We moved the baby from our bed to the cot.

distressed.example: If your baby becomes distressed, go back to the comfort baseline.

aghast.example: She was aghast at the idea of sleeping alone.

arrangement.definition: A plan or agreement about how to do something.
arrangement.example: They made an arrangement about bedtime.

volition.definition: Your own free choice; doing something because you want to.
volition.example: She started going to bed of her own volition.

cosiness.definition: A warm, comfortable, and safe feeling.
cosiness.example: The child's bed was full of cosiness and warmth.

reluctant.definition: Not willing to do something; hesitant.
reluctant.example: The child was reluctant to go to bed.

desperation.definition: A feeling of having no hope and being willing to try anything.
desperation.example: Bedtime should not be about loneliness and desperation.

encouragement.definition: Words or actions that give someone hope and confidence.
encouragement.example: With encouragement, the child learned to sleep alone.

punishment.definition: Something bad that happens to someone because they did something wrong.
punishment.example: Punishment does not help children learn good habits.

tricked.definition: Fooled or deceived into doing something.
tricked.example: Children who feel tricked lose trust in their parents.

manipulated.definition: Controlled or influenced in a clever but unfair way.
manipulated.example: No child should feel manipulated into sleeping.

enhance.definition: To make something better or stronger.
enhance.example: Good communication can enhance your bond with your child.

implement.definition: To start using a plan or system.
implement.example: Try to implement small changes one step at a time.

disempowering.definition: Taking away someone's power or ability to do things.
disempowering.example: Doing everything for your child can be disempowering.

comfort zone.definition: The area where you feel safe and not worried.
comfort zone.example: Nudge children to the edge of their comfort zone, not beyond.

guidance.definition: Help and advice about what to do.
guidance.example: Children need gentle guidance from their parents.
